---
title: "Why Statistical Learning?"
subtitle: "Week 1 — Motivation, Importance, and How It Works"
author: "Ho-min Park & Hwan"
date: 2026-07-10
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    slide-number: c/t
    center: false
    transition: fade
    footer: "ML Summer Class · Week 1"
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
---

# Welcome {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

::: notes
Quick intros, logistics (Fridays 2-4pm Central, 7 weeks), and set expectations:
read before class, we spend class time on discussion + labs.
:::

## About this course

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Instructors:** Ho-min Park & Hwan
- **Format:** 7 Fridays, 2:00–4:00 PM Central
- **Textbook:** *An Introduction to Statistical Learning*, Python edition (ISLP)
- **Style:** short conceptual recap + hands-on Python labs
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Read the chapter **before** class
- Class time = discussion + labs, not lecture
- Every week pairs theory with a runnable notebook
:::
::::

::: notes
Emphasize the flipped-classroom structure — the condensed 7-week pace only
works if people show up having read the chapter.
:::

# Why Are We Doing This? {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Data is everywhere — decisions aren't automatically good

[Every field now collects more data than any person can look at by eye.]{.hl}
The bottleneck has shifted from *collecting* data to *turning it into
decisions*. That's exactly the gap statistical learning fills.

::: {.fragment}
> "Statistical learning refers to a vast set of tools for understanding data."
> — James, Witten, Hastie, Tibshirani, Taylor (ISLP, Ch. 1)
:::

## Three examples from the textbook — one shape

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
### Wage data
Predict a worker's **wage** from age, education, and year. A continuous
number we want to estimate as accurately as possible.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
### Stock market data
Predict whether the market goes **up or down** tomorrow from recent
returns. A category, not a number.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
### Gene expression data
No target to predict at all — just find **structure** (clusters of similar
cell types) hiding in thousands of measurements.
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
Regression, classification, clustering — different questions, same
underlying machinery. That machinery is what this course teaches.
:::

## Why *you*, specifically

- These methods show up **inside** the tools you already use: recommendation
  systems, medical risk scores, fraud detection, A/B tests, genomics
  pipelines.
- Being able to read a model's assumptions (and know when they break) is
  what separates *using* a black box from *trusting* one.
- The skills transfer directly to the Python data-science stack
  (`scikit-learn`, `statsmodels`) you'll use outside this course.

# Why It Is Important {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## It's the common language of modern data work

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
- **Science** — genomics, astrophysics, climate models
- **Medicine** — diagnosis support, survival/risk prediction
- **Business** — demand forecasting, pricing, churn
- **Technology** — search ranking, spam filtering, the models behind LLMs
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{=html}
<div class="stat-card">
  <i class="fa-solid fa-diagram-project"></i>
  <div>
    <strong>One toolkit, many domains.</strong>
    <p>The same bias-variance trade-off that governs a linear regression
    also governs a neural network. Learn the trade-off once.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```
:::
::::

## Getting it wrong is expensive

- An **overfit** model looks great on the data you trained it on and fails
  the moment it meets new data — silently, until it's in production.
- An **overly rigid** model misses real signal and gives you false
  confidence in a simple story.
- Statistical learning gives you the *vocabulary and diagnostics*
  (train/test split, cross-validation, bias-variance) to tell which
  mistake you're making — and fix it.

::: notes
This is the seed for Chapter 2's bias-variance discussion and Chapter 5's
cross-validation — plant the idea now, they'll see the math for it soon.
:::

# How It Works {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## The basic setup

We assume there's some relationship between an input $X$ and an output $Y$:

$$Y = f(X) + \epsilon$$

::: {.fragment}
- $f$ — the **true, unknown** systematic relationship we want to learn
- $\epsilon$ — random noise, independent of $X$, mean zero
- **Statistical learning = a set of approaches for estimating $f$**
:::

::: {.fragment}
Everything this course covers — linear regression, trees, neural networks —
is a different strategy for estimating the same $f$.
:::

## Two reasons to estimate $f$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Prediction
We only care about the output. $f$ can be a "black box" as long as
$\hat{Y} = \hat{f}(X)$ is accurate.

*e.g. will this loan default?*
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Inference
We want to understand **how** $Y$ changes with $X$: which predictors
matter, and how. $f$ must stay interpretable.

*e.g. which drug dosage actually helps?*
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
Most real projects are a mix of both — and the mix determines which method
you should reach for.
:::

## Two kinds of learning problems

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Supervised
Every observation has a **labeled outcome** $Y$. We fit $f$ to match
inputs to known answers.

Regression (numeric $Y$) · Classification (categorical $Y$)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Unsupervised
No labeled outcome — only inputs $X$. We look for structure: groups,
patterns, lower-dimensional summaries.

Clustering · Dimension reduction
:::
::::

## The trade-off you'll see all course long

```{=html}
<div class="tradeoff">
  <div class="tradeoff-item">
    <i class="fa-solid fa-bullseye"></i>
    <strong>Flexible models</strong>
    <p>Trees, splines, deep nets. Fit the training data closely — risk
    overfitting to its noise.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="tradeoff-vs">vs</div>
  <div class="tradeoff-item">
    <i class="fa-solid fa-ruler"></i>
    <strong>Simple models</strong>
    <p>Linear regression, LDA. Easy to interpret, more stable — risk
    missing real structure.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.fragment}
Choosing well — and *measuring* the choice instead of guessing — is the
prediction-accuracy / interpretability trade-off at the heart of Ch. 2, and
it recurs in every later chapter.
:::

## Where the next 7 weeks go

| Week | Focus |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Foundations** — what we just covered |
| 2 | Regression & classification: fitting $f$ to data |
| 3 | Resampling & regularization: trusting your estimate of $f$ |
| 4 | Beyond straight lines: splines, trees |
| 5–6 | Support vector machines, deep learning |
| 7 | Survival analysis, unsupervised learning, multiple testing |

Every week's lab makes the ideas from that week's slides concrete in Python.

# Getting Started {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Before next Friday

1. Read **ISLP Ch. 1–2** (`week1/ch01-02-intro-statistical-learning.pdf`)
2. Set up a Python environment with `jupyter`, `numpy`, `pandas`,
   `scikit-learn`, `statsmodels`, and the `ISLP` package
3. Skim the Ch. 2 lab — *Introduction to Python* — and try running it
4. Bring questions; we'll spend class time on discussion + the lab, not
   re-reading slides

## Questions?

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 2em;"}
[See you Friday.]{.hl}
:::

::: notes
Leave time here for open Q&A before wrapping.
:::
